title: David Hockney, RIP: See the Evolution of His Pop-Art Swimming-Pool Masterpiece A Bigger Splash (1967)
description:
in Art, History |   	June 15th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

In a way, it always made sense that one of the most mem┬нo┬нrable┬аvisu┬нal dis┬нtil┬нla┬нt
body:
in Art, History |   	June 15th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

In a way, it always made sense that one of the most mem┬нo┬нrable┬аvisu┬нal dis┬нtil┬нla┬нtions of┬аSouth┬нern Cal┬нi┬нfor┬нnia life would have been paint┬нed by an Eng┬нlish┬нman.┬аThe purest appre┬нci┬нa┬нtion for the wide-open lifestyle choic┬нes, freestyle built envi┬нron┬нment, unre┬нpen┬нtant pri┬нvate wealth, and high-wattage sun┬нshine of Los Ange┬нles тАФ espe┬нcial┬нly as it was exag┬нger┬нat┬нed, and indeed mythol┬нo┬нgized, in mid-twen┬нti┬нeth cen┬нtu┬нry pop┬нu┬нlar cul┬нture тАФ could only be felt by some┬нone┬аfrom an infi┬нnite┬нly more tra┬нdi┬нtion┬нal, strait┬нened, and damp part of the world. David Hock┬нney, who died last week, was┬нnтАЩt just an Eng┬нlish┬нman but a north┬нern Eng┬нlish┬нman, who would have grown up sur┬нround┬нed by the kind of atti┬нtudes sat┬нi┬нrized in the тАЬFour York┬нshire┬нmenтАЭ sketch made famous by Mon┬нty Python. Lit┬нtle won┬нder he fell in love with the newest city of the New World.
Hock┬нney gave that many artis┬нtic forms over decades of his long life and career. Prac┬нti┬нcal┬нly any┬нone who knows his name can rec┬нog┬нnize A Big┬нger Splash, from 1967, a both idyl┬нlic and faint┬нly eerie depic┬нtion of some┬нone hav┬нing just plunged into the swim┬нming pool behind what now looks like a clas┬нsic тАЬmid┬нcen┬нtu┬нry mod┬нernтАЭ home accent┬нed with palm trees.
But few┬нer can call to mind the works from which it evolved, A Lit┬нtle Splash and The Splash, both of which Hock┬нney paint┬нed the pre┬нvi┬нous year; all togeth┬нer, they con┬нsti┬нtute a series orig┬нi┬нnal┬нly inspired by a pho┬нto┬нgraph on the cov┬нer of a swim┬нming-pool main┬нte┬нnance guide from the late fifties. You can see the three paint┬нings put in con┬нtext in the Sothe┬нbyтАЩs video at the top of the post, which reveals how Hock┬нneyтАЩs image grew more abstract┬нed, and more Los Ange┬нlized, with each iter┬нa┬нtion.


	

When it came time to paint┬аthe third ver┬нsion, Hock┬нney first built up its arrange┬нment of house, pool, div┬нing board, and sky with blocks of flat (if char┬нac┬нter┬нis┬нti┬нcal┬нly bright) col┬нor. He then grad┬нu┬нal┬нly nudged these shapes toward rep┬нre┬нsen┬нta┬нtion by adding detail. Dis┬нcussing the mak┬нing of the paint┬нing lat┬нer in life, he liked to men┬нtion how much time he spent on the splash alone: a full week, at least, to ren┬нder an event that lasts no longer than a sec┬нond or two. There would be more Hock┬нney swim┬нming pools, each evoca┬нtive in its own way, none more expen┬нsive than the near┬нly pho┬нto┬нre┬нal┬нis┬нtic Por┬нtrait of an Artist (Pool with Two Fig┬нures), from 1971, which went for $90.3 mil┬нlion at ChristieтАЩs in 2018. But it was only┬аA Big┬нger Splash┬аthat went on to adorn the cov┬нer of Reyn┬нer Ban┬нhamтАЩs Los Ange┬нles: The Archi┬нtec┬нture of Four Ecolo┬нgies, still one of the most per┬нcep┬нtive books about that city тАФ and one writ┬нten, nat┬нu┬нral┬нly, by anoth┬нer besot┬нted Brit.
Relat┬нed Con┬нtent:
David Hock┬нney Shows Us His Sketch Book, Page by Page
Watch David Hock┬нney Paint with Light, Using the Quan┬нtel Paint┬нbox Graph┬нics Sys┬нtem (1986)
29 Sketch┬нbooks by Renowned Artist Richard Diebenko┬нrn, Con┬нtain┬нing 1,045 Draw┬нings, Now Freely View┬нable Online
How West Mag┬нa┬нzine Cre┬нat┬нed a South┬нern-Cal┬нi┬нfor┬нnia Pop-Cul┬нture Aes┬нthet┬нic with the Help of Mil┬нton Glaser, Gahan Wil┬нson, and Oth┬нers (1967тАУ1972)
Art Trips: Vis┬нit the Art of Cities Around the World, from Los Ange┬нles & Lon┬нdon, to Venice and New York
When Hen┬нri Matisse Was 83 Years Old, He CouldnтАЩt Go to His Favorite Swim┬нming Pool, So He Cre┬нat┬нed a Swim┬нming Pool as a Work of Art
Based in Seoul,┬аCol┬нin┬аMarshall┬аwrites and broad┬нcasts on cities, lan┬нguage, and cul┬нture. HeтАЩs the author of the newslet┬нter┬аBooks on Cities┬аas well as the books┬аэХЬъ╡н ьЪФьХ╜ ъ╕ИьзА┬а(No Sum┬нma┬нriz┬нing Korea) and┬аKore┬нan Newtro.┬аFol┬нlow him on the social net┬нwork for┬нmer┬нly known as Twit┬нter at┬а@colinmarshall.

title: The Splendid Book Design of the 1946 Edition of GibbonтАЩs Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire
description:
in Books, Design |   	June 15th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
In 1929, the book pub┬нlish┬нer George Macy found┬нed The Lim┬нit┬нed Edi┬нtions Club (LEC),
body:
in Books, Design |   	June 15th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
In 1929, the book pub┬нlish┬нer George Macy found┬нed The Lim┬нit┬нed Edi┬нtions Club (LEC), an imprint tasked with pub┬нlish┬нing fine┬нly illus┬нtrat┬нed lim┬нit┬нed edi┬нtions of clas┬нsic books. In┬аthe years to come, Macy worked with artists like Matisse and Picas┬нso, and pho┬нtog┬нra┬нphers like┬аEdward West┬нon, to pro┬нduce books with artis┬нtic illus┬нtra┬нtions on their inner pages. And some┬нtimes The Lim┬нit┬нed Edi┬нtions Club┬аeven turned its design focus to oth┬нer parts of the book. Take for exam┬нple this 1946┬аedi┬нtion of Edward Gib┬нbonтАЩs The Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire┬аand its pret┬нty amaz┬нing spine design.
Cre┬нat┬нed by Clarence P. Hor┬нnung, the design cap┬нtures the essence of Gib┬нbonтАЩs clas┬нsic, show┬нing Roman pil┬нlars pro┬нgres┬нsive┬нly crum┬нbling as your eyes move from Vol┬нume┬а1 to Vol┬нume┬а7. George Macy lat┬нer called the col┬нlec┬нtion, which also fea┬нtures illus┬нtra┬нtions by the great 18th-cen┬нtu┬нry print┬нmak┬нer┬аGio┬нvan┬нni Bat┬нtista Pirane┬нsi, тАЬthe most her┬нculean labor of our career.тАЭ
Find more infor┬нma┬нtion about this 1946 edi┬нtion here. Or, if you have deep pock┬нets, pur┬нchase a copy here.
Note: an ear┬нli┬нer ver┬нsion of this post appeared on our site in June 2015.
If you would like to sup┬нport the mis┬нsion of Open Cul┬нture, con┬нsid┬нer mak┬нing┬аa dona┬нtion to our site. ItтАЩs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your con┬нtri┬нbu┬нtions will help us con┬нtin┬нue pro┬нvid┬нing the best free cul┬нtur┬нal and edu┬нca┬нtion┬нal mate┬нri┬нals to learn┬нers every┬нwhere. You can con┬нtribute through Pay┬нPal, Patre┬нon, and Ven┬нmo (@openculture). Thanks!
Relat┬нed Con┬нtent:
55 Cov┬нers of Vin┬нtage Phi┬нlos┬нo┬нphy, Psy┬нchol┬нo┬нgy & Sci┬нence Books Come to Life in a Short Ani┬нma┬нtion
How Rome Began: The His┬нto┬нry As Told by Ancient His┬нto┬нri┬нans
157 Ani┬нmat┬нed Min┬нi┬нmal┬нist Mid-Cen┬нtu┬нry Book Cov┬нers

title: The .ART Award Honors the Story Behind the Artwork
description: тАЬThe question that has always stayed with me is the one the market rarely asks: what happened in the studio before the work arrived? The sketches
body:
тАЬThe question that has always stayed with me is the one the market rarely asks: what happened in the studio before the work arrived? The sketches abandoned, the ideas reconsidered, the moment something became itself?тАЭ
That is Shlomi Rabi, founder of Bridgewell Arts and a former auction-house specialist, describing what he is now looking for in his new role on the jury of the .ART Award. The global art prize opened for applications this May to mark the 10th anniversary of the .ART domain.
The .ART AwardтАЩs format follows from his question. Every artist who applies builds a working archive in the process: their bio, their work, the story behind it, gathered in one place on a .ART domain. тАЬWe rarely get to see how an idea becomes a work of art,тАЭ says jury member Akanksha Ballaney, Senior Director of Business Development at Artsy and Artnet. тАЬThis award changes that by making the journey as important as the destination.тАЭ
Alongside Rabi and Ballaney, the 10-person international panel includes Pulitzer-winning art critic Jerry Saltz (New York Magazine), poet and digital artist Sasha Stiles (co-founder of theVERSEverse and the artist behind A Living Poem at MoMA), media arts curator Regina Harsanyi (Museum of the Moving Image), Dean Phelus (American Alliance of Museums, Museum┬аMagazine), Gijs Stork (Ch├вteau du Fresne), Laurent Mo├пsi (Whitewall Magazine), Leo Crane (AIFA Ventures), and Irina Tarsis (Center for Art Law).
Stiles, whose own practice spans poetry, code, and large language models, frames her interest plainly: тАЬIтАЩm fascinated by the inner workings of the artistтАЩs studio, the layers of inspiration and influence and craft, the process behind an artwork.тАЭ
A salon at Ch├вteau du Fresne, the .ART Award 2026 residency near Paris, France.
Artist Claudia Pons Bohman at Anfitrion, the .ART Award 2026 residency in Marbella, Spain.
The total prize value for the .ART Award exceeds $50,000, including a $15,000 Grand Prize, residencies at Ch├вteau du Fresne in France and Anfitrion in Spain, an editorial feature in Whitewall Magazine, and a premium .ART domain valued at $10,000.
Applications┬аfor the .ART Award close on┬а November 1, 2026, with winners to be announced on December 3 during Art Basel Miami. It is free to enter and open to artists worldwide working in any discipline.┬а
Apply now at award.art.
Sasha Stiles, тАЬAre you ready for the future?тАЭ (2020). Image courtesy of the artist
Sasha Stiles, тАЬA Blank Garden,тАЭ presented at NFT Paris in 2025. Image courtesy of the artist

schema.headline: The .ART Award Honors the Story Behind the Artwork

title: Annalise Gratovich's Life-Size Woodcuts Meld Nature, Memory, and Ukrainian Heritage
description: In the statement for Annalise GratovichтАЩs solo exhibition, Carrying Things From Home, the gallery poses a couple of questions: тАЬWhen war, displace
body:
In the statement for Annalise GratovichтАЩs solo exhibition, Carrying Things From Home, the gallery poses a couple of questions: тАЬWhen war, displacement, and migration sever familial and cultural ties, how do we sustain a sense of self and ancestral connection? How do we hybridize in a new homeland?тАЭ For the artist, who is based in Austin and runs High Low Print Co., her familyтАЩs history informs a printmaking practice that explores deeply personal and even spiritual links to land, home, and a sense of belonging.
Hecho a Mano presents eight large-scale, hand-carved woodblock prints in Carrying Things From Home, which have been painstakingly created between 2014 and 2025. Gratovich meticulously carves totemic, matryoshka-like figures that stand nearly life-size with headdresses of flora and fauna and garments that resemble embroidered Ukrainian textiles.
тАЬThe FoolтАЭ carving
The series taps into GratovichтАЩs ancestral history, nodding to the tumult of World War II when her grandparents fled Ukraine in the cover of night with their sonтАФthe artistтАЩs fatherтАФand could only take what they could physically carry. тАЬWhat was lost or left behind has become a source of mystery and mythology, and through printmaking, I investigate how this absence influences my sense of self and how I value materiality,тАЭ she says.
The blocks themselves can take up to six months to carve, and then working with her team, Gratovich adds thin pieces of colorful paper through chine coll├й. тАЬFor my most recent works in the series, тАШThe HealerтАЩ and тАШThe Fool,тАЩ I prepared close to 2,000 pieces of paper,тАЭ she says. тАЬThey have 69 and 70 pieces of hand dyed paper coloring each woodcut, respectively.тАЭ
GratovichтАЩs serene figures coalesce with nature and symbolic objects, such as musical instruments and animals, reflected in titles like тАЬThe Builder,тАЭ who holds a raptor, and тАЬThe Healer,тАЭ who balances a swirling, cosmic flow of energy. And from carved block to expansive print, the process requires precision and a few helping hands. тАЬThereтАЩs a community aspect thatтАЩs engaging and funтАФmy big woodcuts require a team of four people to print,тАЭ the artist tells Colossal. тАЬWe use a printing press that is over four by eight feet, and everyone is integral in the printing process.тАЭ
Carrying Things From Home continues through June 28 in Santa Fe. Gratovich is also preparing for a residency at Hello Print Friend Studios in Chang Mai, Thailand, after which sheтАЩll be the keynote speaker at the Guild of BookworkersтАЩ Standards of Excellence 2026 conference. That takes place in Austin, where she also has a studio at the Canopy Austin complex, which is open by appointment. See more on the artistтАЩs Instagram. 
Pulling тАЬThe HealerтАЭ
тАЬThe FoolтАЭ (2025), woodcut with hand-dyed chine coll├й, 71 x 41 inches. Photo by Agave Print
Detail of тАЬThe Fool.тАЭ Photo by Agave Print
Adding paper to тАЬThe FoolтАЭ
Adding paper to тАЬThe FoolтАЭ
тАЬThe BuilderтАЭ (2017), woodcut with hand-dyed chine coll├й, 71 x 41 inches. Photo by Agave Print
тАЬThe HunterтАЭ (2024), woodcut with hand-dyed chine coll├й, 71 x 41 inches. Photo by Agave Print
тАЬThe HealerтАЭ carving

title: Maine College of Art & Design Announces 2026 MFA Visiting Artist Summer Lecture Series
description:
Announcement
                Graduate candidates work with a roster of renowned visiting artists during an eight-week summer intensiv
body:
Announcement
                Graduate candidates work with a roster of renowned visiting artists during an eight-week summer intensive for MECA&DтАЩs MFA in Studio Art.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Colin Self, Siblings (Elation VI), performed at MoMA PS1 in 2018 (photo M. Baranova)
    
        
            During the eight-week summer intensive of the Master of Fine Arts in Studio Art (MFA) program at Maine College of Art & Design (MECA&D), MFA graduate candidates come to Portland, Maine, to bond as an artistic cohort while exploring the interdisciplinary conversation that will challenge and inform the art created throughout their program. Central to this summer intensive is the accelerated professional development that our artist candidates receive from visiting artists who are on-site each week for lectures and studio visits.We are pleased to announce the participants in the 2026 Summer Visiting Artist Lecture Series, running June 24 through August 5:Nyugen E. Smith is an interdisciplinary artist whose work investigates how stories are formed, transmitted, and disrupted within Black cultural life.┬аGarrick Imatani is an interdisciplinary artist who uses performance, functional objects, or interaction to draw attention to oneтАЩs embodied subjectivity.┬аThis lecture is in collaboration with the┬аInstitute of Contemporary Art at MECA&D.┬аColin Self is an artist, composer, and puppeteer who creates music, performances, and new systems for interfacing with the human spirit.Jeffry Mitchell is a self-described тАЬgay folk artistтАЭ whose primary medium is ceramic, drawing on traditions around the globe.Jess Perlitz's work is informed by our formations of landscape and the bodyтАЩs place within it.┬аThis lecture is in collaboration with the┬аInstitute of Contemporary Art at MECA&D.┬аAllison Schulnik's practice spans the mediums of painting, sculpture, animation, and dance.Taraneh Fazeli is an Iranian-American curator and cultural organizer.Gail SpaienтАЩs studio practice centers around the idea that a painting is a site of connection.Crist├│bal Mart├нnez, PhD, is an artist, digital media systems designer, and publishing scholar.Liz Collins is a New York-based artist and designer whose work moves fluidly between fine art and design.┬аIn 2025, the MFA Visiting Artists Lecture Series featured individuals such as Pap Souleye Fall, Faythe Levine, Yoko Inoue, and more.To learn more about the opportunity to work with world-renowned faculty, visiting artists, and graduate advisors, visit meca.edu/mfa.

title: Lesbian Art Novels You Should Read
description:
Books Newsletter
                Sarah Schulman's fiction about lesbian artists, Vaginal Davis's new catalog, and more.
body:
Books Newsletter
                Sarah Schulman's fiction about lesbian artists, Vaginal Davis's new catalog, and more.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            Sarah Schulman speaks as she writes тАФ with candor and a clear-eyed sense of justice. IтАЩve long admired the New York City scholar, organizer, and author, but I finally feel like I understand a core component of her lifeтАЩs work: fiction writing. Read our conversation on the world that shaped her multifaceted practice and numerous novels, which largely center on lesbian protagonists that the publishing industry still ignores.Also this Pride month, we recommend books on the radical work of trans artist Vaginal Davis, the bicoastal Chinatowns that inspired Martin Wong, the intertwined lives of Paul Thek and Peter Hujar, and an early-19th-century lesbian couple in Vermont brought into full relief by graphic novelist Tillie Walden (who is giving a talk tonight in New York City with Hyperallergic contributor Alexis Clements). Check out the full list for your next read!


	
		
			Sarah SchulmanтАЩs Four Decades of Lesbian Fiction
		
	
	
		In her nearly seven decades in the city, the novelist, playwright, and filmmaker has co-founded the Dyke March, conducted 187 interviews with members of the AIDS Coalition to Unleash Power (ACT UP), reported on the HIV/AIDS epidemic, and drawn pedagogy from Audre Lorde, who was her college professor. Though widely recognized for her documentaries and nonfiction books, Schulman identifies first as a novelist; several of her works of fiction focus on lesbian characters and reflect the world of queer poets, artists, and activists she grew up around. | Lakshmi Rivera Amin
	
	
		Read More
	

From Our Critics

	
		
			The World That Held Peter Hujar and Paul Thek
		
	
	
		The late photographer Peter Hujar and visual artist Paul Thek keep showing up these days. And now we have Andrew DurbinтАЩs thoughtfully rendered dual biography of these lovers and friends: The Wonderful World That Almost Was: A Life of Peter Hujar and Paul Thek. 
          This is a biography of two artists coming into being, witnessing each other, building careers, and moving through the complexities of a relationship that began as lovers, confidantes, and advocates, but shifted over time to include longing, resentment, and an inability to connect.
This is neither a happily-ever-after romance nor an entirely tragic tale, though both men died of AIDS in the late 1980s. Instead, it feels more grounded in daily reality, more nuanced and ever-changing, holding the full complexity of queer relations with care and understanding. As a dyke, I particularly cherished this portrait of queer entanglement characterized by duration and valences beyond the sexual or romantic. | Alexis Clements
	
	
		Read More
	

Guide

	
		
			7 Art Books You Should Read This Pride Month
		
	
	
		If youтАЩve watched and loved Paris Is Burning, the iconic 1990 documentary about Black and Latinx ballroom culture in Harlem, this is the reading list for you. This month, delve into new books that highlight queer and trans artists тАФ past and present тАФ who have always shaped the realms of visual art and culture.
	
	
		Read More
	

Excerpt
ICYMI
                  
                      
                        Books Newsletter
                      
                      
                        Newsletter

title: Remembering David Hockney
description:
Daily Newsletter
                The British painter who transformed the everyday, and 25 art shows for America 250.
body:
Daily Newsletter
                The British painter who transformed the everyday, and 25 art shows for America 250.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            British artist David Hockney died at age 88 last week, leaving an oasis-shaped void in our collective imagination. In sun-bleached paintings of California swimming pools, psychological portraits, and later in collages and digital works, he wielded color like few artists before or since and depicted gay relationships with sensitivity and care. Today, Associate Editor Lisa Yin Zhang remembers the work of the prolific, beloved artist and тАЬrestless experimenter.тАЭAs July rapidly approaches and тАЬAmerica 250тАЭ celebrations shift into high gear, Staff Reporter Rhea Nayyar has a guide to must-see art exhibitions that challenge, reframe, and unsettle the history of the United States. More as always, including critic Eileen GтАЩSell on a new 4K restoration of the iconic 1996 film I Shot Andy Warhol.тАФLakshmi Rivera Amin, Associate Editor

	
		
			Painter David Hockney, Who Made the Everyday Otherworldly, Dies at 88 
		
	
	
		Best known for paintings that imbued the everyday with an otherworldly stillness, psychologically precise portraits, and crystalline pool scenes, Hockney also explored printmaking, photography тАФ even stage design for ballet and opera тАФ across his prolific career of more than half a century. He was a restless experimenter, using computer graphics in his work as early as the 1980s and exploring digital painting on his iPad late in life.
      
      He was a pioneer of LGBTQ+ rights тАФ one of the first popular artists to create work depicting gay relationships, and one of the few to publicly denounce censorship of queer imagery. | Lisa Yin Zhang
	
	
		Read More
	


                    
                    
                    
                        
                            Make Art. Build a Career. Enroll today.Whether you're launching an artistic career or returning to a creative passion, PAFA helps you develop the skills to take your work further. Study drawing, painting, printmaking, sculpture, and digital arts in a studio-based environment led by practicing artists. Full-time and part-time options available.┬а
                        
                        
                            Learn more
                        
                    
            NewsThe Knicks and Spurs game gets an arts wager: San AntonioтАЩs Department of Arts and Culture challenged the NYC Department of Cultural Affairs to a friendly competition ahead of Game 5 on Saturday night.The United States at 250
From Our Critics

                    
                    
                    
                        
                            A Kind of Paradise: Reclaiming Colonial-Era Photography Through Contemporary ArtAt Museum Rietberg, 20 global artists transform colonial photographs into new narratives of memory, identity, and resistance.
                        
                        
                            Learn more
                        
                    
            
"Learning how to communicate about difficult subjects is part of how we move toward a better place. People who know me know that I love debate, and that I have worked with high school students to develop these skills. So I make this claim with some practical experience: We can survive talking with one another about hard issues, even issues that pain us. And we are better for it. Being more expansive with others does not mean abandoning our principles. It means approaching people with courtesy and openness, listening, and allowing the conversation to continue. That kind of openness keeps imagination alive."Melanie Cohn on тАЬThey Want to Control Our ImaginationтАЭFrom the Archive
                  
                      
                        Daily Newsletter
                      
                      
                        Newsletter
